observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to take up the reclassification of costs of completed infrastructure projects to the appropriate asset account based on Certificate of Completion and to record the necessary depreciation attributed to the completed projects; and
Municipal Accountant to review the transactions recorded under the CIP accounts and prepare the required CIPLC for each project to ensure proper monitoring of the projects in progress.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct a comprehensive review of all PPE accounts to identify items that fall below the ₱50,000.00 threshold and prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to charge the issued tangible items acquired in CY 2022 onwards and in prior years to the Prior Period Adjustment;
Close the corresponding Accumulated depreciation of the PPE accounts having ₱50,000.00 threshold; and
Verify the acquisition cost of each individual asset before recording it in the PPE ledger. The ₱50,000.00 threshold shall be applied on a per-item basis, and items below this amount should be automatically classified under the appropriate expense or semi-expendable accounts.

In view of these observations, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the following officers:
The Municipal Accountant to
Ensure recording immediately the inventory upon delivery, not just upon submission of disbursement vouchers, to provide accurate recognition of inventory on hand;
Use moving average method for inventory costing to provide consistent and accurate expense recognition, accounting for fluctuations in supply costs; and
Record Supply purchases in the Inventory Account, not directly in the Supplies Expense account, to prevent overstating expenses and understating inventory.
For the GSO to
Closely monitor the deliveries of materials and office supplies and implement perpetual inventory system;
Maintain detailed records for each inventory item by tracking the inventory levels in real-time and ensure proper tracking.
Refrain from direct delivery to the End-user instead the GSO be informed on all the deliveries of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to undertake the following courses of actions, to wit:
For the Municipal Treasurer to:
Closely monitor the PCF and replenish the fund when approximately 75% has been used, rather than waiting until it is completely depleted, to ensure smooth operations.
Utilize Petty Cash Vouchers (PCVs) instead of Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) for managing petty cash transactions. Additionally, maintain a report on paid PCVs and keep a Petty Cash Register to ensure accurate tracking.
Immediately halt the use of the PCF for the payment of recurring expenses.
For the Municipal Accountant to:
Follow proper procedures for recording transactions to ensure that petty cash usage is accurately accounted for, in line with the LGU-NGAS Manual.

In view of the audit findings and in order to strengthen the Municipality’s financial management and internal control systems and in compliance with provisions of Section 124 of Presidential Decree No. 1445, Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 32, Chapter 3, Volume I of the Manual of the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Unit (MNGAS for LGUs) and provisions of the National Guidelines on Internal Control Systems (NGICS), we recommended, and the Management agreed to undertake the following actions, to wit:
For the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to instruct the following officers to implement the corrective measures:
For Revenue Collection Clerks and/or Authorized Collecting Officers:
Remit all collections in full to the Municipal Treasurer on a daily basis; and
Accomplish the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) completely in four (4) copies, in accordance with the MNGAS for LGUs.
For Municipal Treasurer:
Ensure that all daily collections are deposited intact with the Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB), in compliance with Section 69(1) of Presidential Decree No. 1445, Section 15 of the NGAS for LGUs, and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
Conduct end-of-day cash on hand reconciliations to identify discrepancies, avoid under or over deposits, and ensure the completeness of collections prior to bank deposit;
Immediately discontinue the practice of utilizing daily collections to pay for agency expenses, as it contravenes established cash management regulations and may result in the disallowance of expenditures during audit;
Review and validate the RCDs submitted by Collecting Officers and the Liquidating Officer. Ensure that all sections are properly accomplished and prepared in four (4) copies, following the required distribution as prescribed by the MNGAS;
Maintain and consolidate monthly RCD summaries in electronic format (e.g., Excel), and submit hard copies to both the Municipal Accountant and the Audit Team to enhance transparency and facilitate monitoring and reconciliation;
Thoroughly examine the issued accountable forms against the corresponding RCDs to detect any omissions, discrepancies, or irregularities; and
Prohibit any alterations in duplicate and triplicate copies. In cases of error, cancel the affected OR and issue a new one in accordance with standard procedures.
For Municipal Accountant:
Utilize the prescribed Cash Receipts Journal (CRJ) format provided in Annex 2 of the MNGAS, ensuring all required fields—such as RCD Number, JEV Number, and Name of Collecting Officer—are accurately accomplished; and
As the Internal Auditor of the Municipality, to review and validate all submissions from the Treasury Office to ensure compliance with relevant laws, accounting standards, and the recommendations provided herein.
We also recommended, the LCE agreed to undertake the following courses of action, to wit:
Designation of Key Personnel:
Formally designate a Liquidating Officer through the issuance of an official office order, to clearly establish accountability and ensure adherence to internal control policies; and
Appoint a separate Petty Cash Custodian within the Treasury Office who is not involved in the receipt of collections, to uphold the principle of segregation of duties and mitigate risks associated with fund mismanagement.
Adoption of Automated RPT System
Prioritize the implementation of the Pangasinan Real Property Tax System (PaRTS) in the Treasury Office to improve the accuracy and integrity of revenue records;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer and Budget Officer for the appropriation of funds for the procurement of computers and printers; and
Facilitate the training of the staff in the Treasury Office, particularly those assigned to the Land Tax Section, to ensure effective utilization of the system and alignment with operational procedures.

We recommended and the management agreed, to strictly adhere to the established procedures for handling Cash Advances, in accordance with Sections 4 and 5 of COA Circular No. 97-002. The Management further committed to undertake the following courses of actions, to wit:
For the Municipal Treasurer:
Separate the cash advances for payroll concerning JO and contractual employees, and use checks for other routine expenses.
Enforce the issuance of cash advances strictly for their intended purposes and avoid granting cash advances for varied payrolls to ensure transparency, accountability, and compliance with government accounting and auditing standards.
Stop paying labor costs for repairs using cash advances; instead, process payments directly to the contractor while following proper procurement procedures.
Prepare and submit a duly certified Monthly Report of Disbursements to the Municipal Accountant, making sure all necessary supporting documents are attached.
For the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare and certify the Cash Disbursement Journal and verify the reports submitted by the Municipal Treasurer.
Ensure and monitor that all cash advances granted are fully liquidated within the prescribed period and by year-end to prevent fund mismanagement.
Additionally, we recommended that Management take appropriate actions to facilitate the immediate liquidation of long-outstanding cash advances. This may include withholding of salaries of concerned officers and employees if they fail to liquidate their existing cash advances after repeated demands.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to undertake the following courses of action and require the Municipality Accountant, to wit:
Verify the validity of Accounts Payable aged two years or more;
Send confirmation letters to the identified claimants or creditors regarding the outstanding balances; and
Revert any payables to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund when it is determined that there are no valid supporting documents.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Provide for the lacking disclosures in the Notes to FS to comply with the disclosure requirement of IPSAS 1 and MNGAS for LGUs.
Appropriately include a sufficient and comprehensive disclosure of Prior Period Adjustments and the Adjustments to Net Revenue Recognized Directly in Net Assets/Equity in the Notes to FS as required under IPSAS 1 and MNGAS for LGUs by providing information on the nature, amount of correction for each line item affected, and a description of how and from when the adjustments has been corrected.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to undertake the following courses of action, to wit:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all accountable officers handling government funds are properly bonded to avoid exposure of government funds to risk of loss or misappropriation pursuant to Section 101 of PD No. 1445 and Section 4.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer and Budget Officer to meet with the LCE to discuss the potential realignment and/or appropriation of funds for the payment of fidelity bonds for unbonded accountable officers.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to undertake the following courses of action:
Instruct the Accounting Office to immediately prepare the General Journal for the relevant periods and ensure that all transactions are recorded accurately in compliance with MNGAS for LGUS; and
Capacitate the staff of the Accounting Office to participate in training sessions on the provisions of MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to undertake the following courses of action, to wit:
The Accounting Office should immediately implement procedures to maintain the necessary registries for obligations, allotments, and disbursements in compliance with MNGAS for LGUS to verify whether the recorded financial activities are adequate and controlled.
Also, the Accounting Office should attend training on the provisions of MNGAS for LGUs, focusing on how to maintain accurate and timely records and reports for monitoring financial transactions.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to strictly meet the submission deadlines for the following reports and accounts to ensure timely review and verification.
We also recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all accounts and reports for succeeding months are submitted to the auditor on or before the 10th day of the following month pursuant to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-06. This will enable a timely audit of transactions and ensure the validity, completeness, and accuracy of the recorded transactions.
Finally, we recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to obtain proof of payment receipt for the disbursements before issuing checks to payees.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to comply with Section 2A.02 of the Municipal Revenue Code of San Fabian to:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the BPLO, to devise measures to validate the declared gross receipts of establishments applying for permits to engage in business, especially those with direct transactions with the LGU; and
Enforce the collection of underpayments, including the applicable surcharges and interest as provided in the Municipal Revenue Code. Non-compliance may necessitate the issuance of a corresponding Notice of Charge.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

2. (a) prepare the correct reconciliation between the actual amounts on a comparable basis as presented in SCBAA and in the SFPer for CY 2022; and

3.  (b) present the disclosure in either the SCBAA or in the Notes to Financial Statements as required by IPSAS 24.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct:

4. a)	Municipal Assessor to process the titling of all parcels of land owned by the Municipal Government, including the completion and submission of all the lacking documentary requirements to facilitate faster processing of transfer of ownership and titling under the name of the Municipal Government; and

5. b)Municipal Assessor and Municipal Budget Officer to allocate sufficient funds for the processing of transfer of ownership in favor of the LGU.

• For the MHO or concerned personnel to furnish the Municipal Accountant with copies of Philhealth claim documents in order to set up the Due from GOCCs account – Receivable from PhilHealth. This will certainly aid in the monitoring of claims pending payment by PhilHealth;

Management appreciated the audit recommendations and has committed to immediately comply them by assigning a personnel who will reconcile the noted discrepancies before effecting the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare a detailed reconciliation statement which accurately aligns the actual amounts as presented in the SCBAA with the figures indicated in the Statement of Financial Performance for CY 2023; and
Disclose the information on the reconciliation between the budgeted and actual figures in the Notes to Financial Statements in compliance with IPSAS Nos. 1 and 24.
The audit recommendations are well taken by the Management and they have committed to submit a corrected and reconciled SCBAA.

We recommended the following measures to Management:
a. 	The Municipal Treasurer should ensure that all accountable officers handling government funds are properly bonded to avoid exposure of government funds to risk of loss or misappropriation pursuant to Section 101 of PD No. 1445 and Section 66 of GAAM Volume I.
b. 	The Local Chief Executive should immediately confer with the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer for possible realignment and/or appropriation of funds for the payment of fidelity bonds of un-bonded accountable officers.
c. 	Management should refrain from designating Job Order personnel as Market Collectors.  However, considering the issue on lack of manpower, it is suggested, for the meantime, that the Market Inspector strictly supervise and monitor the activities of the concerned Job Order personnel and closely coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer in order to safeguard government funds against possible loss or misappropriation.
Management has committed to comply with the audit recommendations.  It was further stated that they will prioritize the bonding of collectors holding permanent position.  However, they justified the need to utilize the services of job order personnel due to manpower shortages.  Nevertheless it was assured that they will be subjected to direct supervision of the Municipal Treasurer effective immediately.
It was also mentioned that Management will exert efforts to resolve related issues on lack of manpower and plantilla positions at the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to:
Ensure that all cash advances granted are fully liquidated within the prescribed period and at year-end;
Granting of cash advances be limited strictly to accountable officers without existing and unliquidated balances; and
See to it that any unexpended balances are immediately refunded.
It was also recommended for Management to institute proper action that will cause immediate liquidation of long outstanding cash advances, such as withholding of salaries of concerned officers and employees after demand for the liquidation of existing cash advances remained unheeded.
The Management averred that all unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2023, have been fully liquidated as of February 2024 and the necessary documents will be provided to the Audit Team for validation/verification.
Further, it was assured that close monitoring of cash advances will be made to prevent recurrence of the noted observation.  The Municipal Accountant was instructed to issue demand letters to erring officials and employees to enforce liquidation of outstanding cash advances which are already due for liquidation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to consolidate requisitions and procurements in order to avoid splitting of contracts and to ensure compliance with the requirement on posting of procurement opportunities in PhilGEPS as provided for under Items IV(B) and IV(E), Annex H of the 2016 revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the BAC revise the RFQ template to ensure that the ABC and other terms and conditions of the contract are indicated therein; and properly accomplish the same in order to provide significant information to prospective bidders/suppliers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to require the suppliers, either losing or winning ones, to submit the required documents as prescribed under Appendix A, Annex H of the revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ascertain whether the agency is transacting with suppliers of known qualifications only.
Management commented that in the evaluation of bids, the BAC has exercised leniency due to the reason that there is only one bidder. Nevertheless, it was assured that the recommendations will be complied with to ensure that the municipality is transacting with suppliers of known qualifications only.

We recommended that the BAC immediately stop the practice of procuring supplies and/or items from non-PhilGEPS-registered suppliers.
Henceforth, we recommended that the BAC see to it that only qualified suppliers are requested to tender their price quotations and/or awarded with contracts in compliance with Section 8.5.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and Section J of the Guidelines on for Shopping and Small Value Procurement.
Management claimed that PhilGEPS registration is discretionary on the action of the procuring entity.  Nonetheless, it was committed that non-PhilGEPS registered suppliers will be barred to participate in the subsequent procurement activities in compliance with the audit recommendations.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
During the exit conference, the Audit Team reiterated that Section 8.5.1 of the revised IRR of RA No. 9184 requires mandatory PhilGEPS registration and did not mention of any qualification, hence compliance is compulsory regardless of the amount involved.
Moreover, it was emphasized that compliance with such requirement is necessary to promote transparency and fair competition.

We recommended the following measures, and Management has agreed to:
Require all suppliers to indicate the brand names and detailed specifications of items being quoted in the price quotation section of the RFQs; and
Ensure that the brand names of items, as quoted by the winning suppliers, are indicated in the POs in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010 and to have a reliable basis in checking the conformity and quality of delivered goods.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to require all contractors of infrastructure-related projects to submit the required certificates of CSHP to ensure compliance with Section 37.2.3(f) of the revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and Section 5 of DOLE DO No. 13, series of 1998 for the safety and health of workers and the public.
During the exit conference, it was justified by the BAC Chairman that they have relied on the Omnibus Sworn Statement of the bidders as regards the contractors’ compliance with the requirement for the CSHP from the DOLE.  Nevertheless, Management assured to strictly adhere to the requirement in compliance with the procurement laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended the following measures to the Local Development Council:
Identify the needs of the different barangays and meticulously and judiciously plan the programs, projects and activities categorized as Various Small Scale Projects funded by the 20% DF in compliance with the DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Circular No. 1; and
Indicate sufficient data in the APP such as name of the projects, project locations and ABC for transparency purposes.
The audit observation was well taken by Management.  It was committed that the concerned LGU officials and employees, particularly the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) members, will be advised to properly coordinate with the target constituencies in order to obtain information on the proposed projects.  Thereafter, prioritization of projects to be implemented will be made to ensure that more important and urgent requests are given preferential attention.
In addition, the Engineering Office was directed to provide technical expertise, assistance and guidance, as well as prepare the necessary program of works and other required documents for the proposed priority small-scale development projects to ensure the feasibility of the project and accuracy of project requirements and specifications so that government funds will not go to waste.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately effect the transfer of the unexpended portion of the 2023 QRF and DRRMF-MOOE from General Fund to Trust Fund pursuant to Section 5.1.10 COA Circular No. 2012-02 dated September 12, 2012.
Likewise, it was recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary accounting entries relative to the transfer of 2023 unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE from General Fund to Trust Fund or provide an adequate disclosure thereof in order to fairly present the balances of the affected accounts in the Financial Statement.
Henceforth, Management was advised to ensure strict compliance with the aforementioned regulations.
Management commented that they have already prepared the necessary accounting entries relative to the transfer of the unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE from the General Fund to Trust Fund last January 31, 2024 as per DV#100-24-01-092 and the actual transfer of funds was made as per check# 95436 dated April 23, 2024, subject to the Audit Team’s validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to immediately revert the unexpended balances of the MOOE to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund in compliance with Section 322 of RA 7160, also known as the Local Government Code of 1991.
We also advised the Municipal Budget Officer to verify the legitimacy of the fund source and classification before certifying as to the existence of the appropriations for expenditures, as well as the remaining balances of MOOE which should be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund at the end of the fiscal year.
Henceforth, it was recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer strictly adhere to the aforementioned regulations.
The Management commented that they will comply with the audit recommendations.  It was further mentioned that once correctly verified, they will prepare a Supplemental Budget for the unappropriated surplus.
During the exit conference, Management informed the Audit Team that they have already drafted a supplemental budget for the unexpended balance.  It was also committed that fund balances will be closely monitored to ensure optimum utilization of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
For the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, as well as all accountable officers concerned, to strictly comply with the timely preparation and submission of Disbursement Vouchers and all other documents pertinent to the agency’s transactions;
For the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to locate the 99 un-submitted DVs and immediately submit the same,  otherwise the corresponding amounts thereof will not be considered as allowable cash items and will form part of the cash shortage since they have been recorded as cash disbursements in the Municipal Treasurer’s cashbooks; and
For the Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of paying transactions that are not supported by DVs and supporting documents, and those not bearing the required approval of the proper officials in order to ensure the validity and propriety of the transactions, to avoid disallowance in audit.
During the exit conference, it was mentioned that all the 99 DVs were already submitted to the Office of the Auditor as of June 2024, but subject to validation and verification of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to establish control mechanisms which will minimize, if not avoid, the incidence of paying salaries which are below the minimum allowed net take home pay, such as limitation on the availment of additional loans.
As regards the other uncontrollable factors which affect the net take home pay of employees in the succeeding payroll months, we recommended that Management submit a justification letter with supporting documents/schedules, if any, to provide easy reference for the Audit Team.
Notwithstanding the foregoing, we recommended strict adherence with Section 58 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act (GAA) for FY 2023 to ensure that adequate funds are available for the basic needs of each employee.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations and adhere to the afore-cited regulation.  It was also mentioned that certifications on the payslips will not be given to employees who are currently receiving minimum net pay of ₱5,000.00 or even those with take home pay that is slightly higher than the minimum net pay, taking into consideration the amount of deductions that could reduce the net take home pay to an amount below the minimum required.
Additionally, the Management assured the Audit Team that they will gradually resolve the issues related to employees whose net take home pay are below the minimum required amount.

We recommended that Management require the Funeral Parlor concerned to immediately refund the overpayment totaling ₱250,000.00, otherwise, the said amount will be disallowed in audit.
Likewise, it was recommended that the amount of ₱60,000.00 be refunded by the concerned officials of the Municipality.
Management committed to coordinate with the owner of the funeral parlor for the refund of ₱250,000.00.  However, as regards the amount of ₱60,000.00, they justified that the assistance was extended to the two beneficiaries due to the following reasons:
The individual who died of a vehicular crash was eventually declared by Department of Health (DOH) as COVID-19 victim, thus considered as eligible for the assistance; and
The other casualty of COVID-19 virus was a former resident of San Fabian.  His relatives residing in the Municipality of San Fabian were the ones who sought assistance.
In view of the difficulties encountered during the time of COVID-19 pandemic, the Management could not afford to turn down the request of people who are in dire need for help.  Thus, for humanitarian consideration, the assistance was immediately extended to the two beneficiaries.  Relatively, Management has requested for the Audit Team’s understanding and consideration, and which the latter has granted during the exit conference.

We recommended that Management adopt the geo-tagging of projects in order to:
provide a more transparent, accurate and reliable report on project accomplishments; and
ensure effective monitoring of government projects to avoid duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects.
The Management recognized the importance of the geo-tagging technology and has committed to adopt the same to obtain an accurate and transparent documentation which will aid in the monitoring and evaluation of project implementation.  However, it was mentioned that geo-tagging of projects in some upland areas is not possible due to lack of internet connection.
As of this writing, Management has started using the technology in documenting the projects at different stages of implementation – before, during and after completion of civil works or infrastructure projects.

We recommended, and Management has agreed to continuously ensure timely remittance of all deducted contributions and withheld taxes to the concerned agencies in adherence with the pertinent laws, rules and regulations and to prevent accumulation of balances and incurrence of corresponding penalties pursuant to PD No. 1445, RA No. 8291, RA No. 9679 and RA No. 7875.

We recommended that the Management to instruct the General Services Officer Designate to monitor the expiration and renewal of each insurance policies of all insurable assets of the Municipality to ensure protection against any additional cost and liabilities that could derive in case of any accidents/fortuitous events in the future.

58. We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit the necessary documents to support the payment of tax deficiencies in order for the Audit Team to conduct verification and validation on the propriety, legality, and validity of the claim, otherwise the amount of P3,026,371.08 will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that in order to ascertain the effectiveness and efficiency of the GAD programs, activities and projects in addressing the significant gender issues and concerns, the Management require the GAD Focal person to:

59. a. Establish and maintain a GAD database that could either be manually operated or developed through software;

60. b. Conduct a gender analysis of their programs and projects through the administration of the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) tool. 61. c. Establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System in order that outcomes of GAD programs, projects and activities be assessed and GAD areas that needs improvement will be properly identified and addressed.

62. d. Submit the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report to COA within the prescribed period for audit purposes.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to take immediate action by following the provisions set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005 pertaining to recognition of impairment loss on the “Other Receivable” account in order to reflect reliable balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended the Management to:

Create an Inventory Committee that will conduct a complete physical count of all Road Networks of the LGU.

Require the GSO Designate and in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to prepare and maintain an Inventory of all Local Roads and a Road Map and thereafter prepare the Report of Local Road Network annually.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to make an entry to recognize completed road network projects prior to CY 2020 including those turned-over by the DPWH; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation and any impairment of the Local Road Network.
observations.managementAction: Due to the on-going pandemic, conduct of complete physical count of all road networks of the municipality were put on hold, however, the management committed to prioritize the completion of the physical count thus current year.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that collections be deposited intact and not later than the next banking day and avoid paying expenses out of the collections in adherence to the above-cited provisions governing collections and deposits of  government funds in order to avoid exposure of funds to possible loss, misappropriation or misuse.

We reiterated our audit recommendation that Management:

Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to require the revenue collectors to regularly remit their collections at the close of each business day pursuant to the provisions of Section 29 of the Manual of NGAS to avoid accumulation of significant amount of money which could possibly result in misuse or loss of public funds.

Make evaluation on the performance of the revenue collectors and consider the imposition of penalties such as filing administrative charges or suspension from work to the erring employees while rewarding the good performing personnel.
observations.managementAction: Due to pandemic and for a humanitarian reason, strict compliance and penalties were not imposed.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Management provide sufficient fund and budget for the fidelity bond premium thru a supplemental budget and thereafter apply for the renewal of the bond for all Accountable Officers to the Bureau of Treasury in accordance with Section 1-1 of PD No. 1145 and Section 7.2 of the Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 in order that the Municipality could make full recovery and/or indemnification in case of loss and/or misappropriation of funds and properties.

We recommended that the BAC and TWG strictly validate the technical requirements submitted by bidder with the Lowest Calculated and Responsive Bid (LCRB)to facilitate the conduct of assessment on the competence and sufficiency of manpower and equipment pledged to each contract to ensure the timely and best quality of work for all the projects of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management direct the BAC Secretariat to post the APP in the GPPB Portal for Emergency Procurement in accordance with the GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 under RA No. 11469 or the Bayanihan to Heal as One Act (“Bayanihan Act”) in order to promote transparency and accountability in its implementation.

We recommended that the Management during the preparation of the Annual Budget for the 20% Development Fund, include only the PPA that are within the enumerated developmental PPA of the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 so that more services/programs for social and development projects will be implemented.

We recommended that the Management establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System in order that outcomes of GAD programs, projects and activities will be assessed and GAD areas that need improvement will be properly identified and addressed.

We also recommended that the GAD Focal Person and Municipal Budget Officer submit the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report to COA within the prescribed period for audit purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the personnel in charge of the Solid Waste Management to carry out strategy to resolve the issue of the size of the trash bins such as setting a manageable volume of the contents of each unit for easier transportation of the same. observations.recommendation:
1.9   We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a restated Financial Statements for the year 2020 as part of the Comparative Financial Statements of the current year for easy and reliable analysis of data for future decision-making and avoid misleading information to the Financial Statement users. In addition, follow IPSAS 1 and 3 for disclosure requirements of financial restatement.
1.10  The Municipal Accountant in her letter reply dated April 18, 2022 acknowledges the audit observation and commits to adhere with the recommendation however as of this writing the Financial Statements were not yet restated.

2.6    We recommended the Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the fair presentation and recognize intangible asset in accordance with IPSAS 31.
2.7  The Municipal Accountant acknowledged the audit observation and already prepared the necessary adjusting entries through journal entry voucher no. 6, 7 and 8 dated February 28, 2022.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to take immediate action by following the provisions set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005 pertaining to recognition of impairment loss on the “Other Receivable” account in order to reflect reliable balances in the Financial Statements.
The Municipal Accountant commented that coordination was already made with the Department of Agriculture for the list of farmers granted with the loan. She further added that once the list is secured, verification and validation will follow before preparation of the necessary adjusting entries to recognize the impairment loss.

In order to avoid exposure of funds to possible loss, misappropriation or misuse, we recommended the Management to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Ensure that collections be deposited intact and not later than the next banking day; and
Refrain paying expenses out of the collections; instead urgent/emergency expenditures amounting to P15,000.00 and below shall be paid from the petty cash fund while expenses above P15,000.00 be paid thru issuance of check.
The Municipal Treasurer acknowledged their weakness as noted in audit and she assured the Audit Team to correct the deficiencies and comply with the audit recommendations.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended the Management to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to strengthen the internal control system in handling accountable forms by implementing the following:
Accountable Forms be kept in a safe or secured storage where access should be limited to an Authorized Personnel, assigned by the Municipal Treasurer as the accountable forms custodian, to control and monitor the purchases, requisitions and issuances of all the accountable forms.
Assign a custodian for the maintenance of a Permanent Record Book for Accountable Forms. The said record book should contain the following column headings:
1) Booklet Number/Quantity (in case of official receipt without money value)
2) Serial Number/Total Value (in case of official receipt with money value)
3) Name (in print) of Accountable Officer to whom issued
4) Signature of Accountable Officer
5) Date received by Accountable Officer
6) Date reported totally used, sold, issued
Require all the Accountable Officers to prepare at the close of each month their RAAF and submit to the Municipal Treasurer for consolidation. The consolidated RAAF together with the individual RAAF should be submitted to the Audit Team on or before the 5th day of the following month.
The management acknowledged and agreed with the audit observations and recommendations.

9.6   We recommended the Management to provide sufficient fund and budget for the fidelity bond premium thru a supplemental budget and thereafter apply for the renewal of the bond for all Accountable Officers to the Bureau of Treasury in accordance with Section 1-1 of PD No. 1145 and Section 7.2 of the Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 in order that the Municipality could make full recovery and/or indemnification in case of loss and/or misappropriation of funds and properties.
9.7   The Municipal Treasurer recognized the observation of the team and has already renewed the fidelity bond of the accountable officers as of March 2022.

10.11 We recommended that the Management:
Require the Accountable Officer to secure approved and signed payroll and other documentary requirements before granting cash advances to avoid excessive funds in the hands of the Accountable Officer; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer and HRMO to coordinate with the Land Bank of the Philippines to employ the use the ATM payroll system for the payment of salaries and wages and other compensation and benefits of Municipal Officials and Employees to promote convenience of all officials and employees, reduce workloads of those involved in payroll payment, avoid the risk of fund loss or misuse and to abide with safety protocols on COVID 19.
10.12  The City Accountant agreed to comply with the audit recommendations. She also confirmed in her letter reply dated April 18, 2022 the attestation of the OIC – Municipal Treasurer during the exit conference last April 7, 2022 that the Municipal Treasurer together with the HRMO are already coordinating  with the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) regarding the ATM payroll system of the Municipality and that they are currently waiting for the approval of the SB Resolution on the Memorandum of Agreement between LGU and LBP.

12.5 We recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE), as Head of the Procuring Entity, to instruct the Inspectorate Team/Project Monitoring Committee to ensure compliance by the contractors on the provisions of DOLE DO No. 13, particularly on the availability of personal protective equipment in order to promote the welfare of every construction workers.
12.6  The management appreciates the audit observation and resolved to conform with the audit recommendation. Accordingly, the Municipal Engineer already had a discussion with the contractor concern.
13.5 The BAC failed to explain the inconsistency and lapses noted in the procurement process which was not in accordance with R.A. 9184.

We recommended that the BAC and TWG strictly validate the technical requirements submitted by bidder with the Lowest Calculated and Responsive Bid (LCRB) to facilitate the conduct of assessment on the competence and sufficiency of manpower and equipment pledged to each contract to ensure the timely and best quality of work for all the projects of the Municipality.
Management agreed to yield with the audit recommendation.

We recommended the Management to instruct the:
BAC Chairman to prescribe and require all the offices/departments to submit a PPMP with uniform/standard format for easy consolidation and preparation of the APP-CSE.
BAC Secretariat to properly consolidate and prepare the APP-CSE, based on the submitted PPMP, using the prescribed form set by the PS-DBM.
General Services Office (GSO) to maintain a price database for all the supplies and materials purchases of the Municipality to be able to set the maximum price ceiling for each items.
15.15  Management assured compliance with the audit recommendation.

16.5 We recommended that the Management require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and BAC Secretariat to ensure confidentiality of the bidding documents prior to submission and opening of bids in order to protect the integrity of the bidding process, promote fair competition among prospective bidders and avail the most advantageous prices for the Municipality.
16.6  The management agreed to adhere with the audit recommendation.
17.12 The Municipal Accountant agreed to abide with the audit recommendation and    ensure that documents will be properly filled up, dated and complete.

18.6  We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to establish control mechanisms not to allow Municipal employees avail loans that will eventually reduce their monthly net take home pay below Five Thousand Pesos (P 5,000.00) as provided in the GAA FY 2021 so that adequate funds is available for the basic needs of each employee.
18.7  The HRMO and Municipal Accountant agreed with the audit recommendation.

19.7  We recommended that in order to control fuel consumption, the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the drivers concerned to properly fill-up the trip tickets and adhere strictly to the guidelines promulgated in the use of government vehicles;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Office of the Auditor the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption within ten (10) days of the succeeding month for validation; and
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt sound internal control measures, procedures and guidelines in the use of fuel such as the use of Requisition Issue Voucher or its equivalent to avoid misuse and wastage of government resources.
19.8    The Municipal Treasurer pledged to conform with the audit recommendations.

20.7  We recommended that in order to create a greater/bigger impact from the projects, programs and activities under the 20% DF, the Management instruct the Local Development Council to:
Meticulously and judiciously plan the PPA’s under the Various Small Scale Projects funded from the 20% DF by identifying the needs of the different barangays as early as the budget preparation stage;
Indicate sufficient data in the APP such as name of the project, estimated cost and location for transparency purposes; and
Strictly comply with the DILG-DBM Joint Circular No 2017-1 that projects identified under 20% DF shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
20.8   Management acknowledged the audit observation and recommendation.

21.7  We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare an accounting entry to:
Transfer the unspent portion of the 2021 QRF and DRRMF-MOOE from General Fund to Trust Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-02 to fairly present the balances of the affected accounts in the Financial Statement or provide an adequate disclosure thereof; and
Recognize expense “Transfers of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds” attributable to year 2020 as adjustment to government equity.
21.8  Accordingly, the Municipal Accountant already transferred the 2021 QRF and DRRMF-MOOE including the excess of 2021 DRRMF-CO from General Fund to Trust Fund amounting to ₱1,273,127.21 as evidenced by DV No. 100-22-02-013, Check No. 863047. Also, necessary accounting entries was already done to recognize expense.

22.2 For the CY 2017 to 2021, the Municipality has invested a total amount of ₱11,931,426.27 for its vision and compliance with RA No. 9003 in the establishment of a sanitary landfill which is considered as the most viable, effective and long-term management of solid waste. These capital expenditures are summarized in Table 13.
Table 13 – Schedule of Capital Outlays for Municipal Dumpsite
22.3  As can be gleaned in the table, the project for the establishment of a landfill is still in progress despite the effectivity of the Act in 2001. To address the problem on waste disposal, the Municipal Government of San Fabian renewed its Disposal Agreement in 2021 with Metro Clark Waste Management Corporation (MCWMC) who owns and operates the engineered sanitary landfill (SLF) at Clark Integrated Waste Management Center which is outside the Province of Pangasinan for a tipping fee of P850.00 per ton. For CY 2021, the Municipality paid an aggregate amount of P1,183,716.22 for the contract.
22.4  However, the facility being availed at present is only a temporary solution and is considered unsustainable which may possibly or suddenly be suspended or terminated just like the case of the Urdaneta SLF where the Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR) ordered for its closure.
22.5  In addition, interview with the Senior Environment Management Specialist and designated Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer (MENRO), disclosed that the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan was returned by the Solid Waste Management Commission due to lack of Material Recovery Facility (MRF) requirement. He also revealed that there are a lot of programs that needs to be done such as the following:
waste segregation at source;
revenue-generating measure to promote viability of the plan; and
coordination with barangays to strengthen solid waste management
22.6  For the proposed sanitary landfill, the LGU has not yet installed the minimum criteria for the establishment of sanitary landfills such as the liners, leachate collection and treatment system, gas control and recovery system and groundwater monitoring well system and covering consisting of soil and geosynthetic materials to protect the waste from long-term contact with the environment. Also, the application for electricity of the site is still in process.
22.7 We recommended that in order to comply with the provisions of RA No. 9003 and to ensure a viable, effective, sustainable and long-term management of solid waste in the LGU the Management instruct the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board the following:
Comply with the Material Recovery Facility (MRF) requirement by the Solid Waste Management Commission so that the 10-year Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan will be approved by the Commission;
Thru the Municipal Engineering Office, establish the minimum criteria for the establishment of sanitary landfill;
Launch a municipal-wide campaign for waste segregation at source
and revenue-generating measure to promote viability of the plan; and
Coordinate with barangays to strengthen solid waste management
22.8 The Management thru the Senior Environmental Management Specialist took note and committed to comply with the audit recommendations. According to him and as evidenced by the certification issued by the DENR Regional Office I, the 10-year SMW Plan of the LGU was already endorsed to the National Solid Waste Management Commission for final evaluation and the DENR RO-I scheduled technical assistance for the development and conceptual design of the SLF.

23.7  We recommended to the Management to require the LDRRMO and the MDPO to provide explanation/justification on the procurement of the ArcGIS wherein there is an available free software used by nearby municipalities and cities with features comparable to the licensed version.
23.8  In response to the AOM, the proponent of the project averred that the promo package availed by the LGU has a commercial value of P11,000,000.00 to P16,000,000.00 when bought individually; hence, the LGU has saved with the contract cost amounting to P1,200,000.00 to P1,500,000.00 only. In addition, the DILG introduced GIS using ArcGIS and afterwards Quantum Geographic Information System came afterwards. Moreover, they provided the list of organizations using ArcGIS.
23.9  Auditor’s rejoinder:
We have taken note of the savings from the procured system as claimed by the MPDC and MDRRM Officer being the proponents/users of the system; however, due to lack of feasibility study, the LGU was deprived of the chance to explore a much cheaper or even cost-free system that will eventually produce comparable results as used by other nearby LGUs. Further, as per verification with some organizations listed as users of ArcGIS commented that they turned down the offer.
